rename-and-remove-tracked-files.label: Rename and Remove Tracked Files
rename-and-remove-tracked-files.summary: Use Git-aware structural operations, review the staged paths, and record a cleanup without losing earlier versions.

sketch1.title: Remove a Path Without Erasing History

sketch3.title: Describe the Cleanup as a Result

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.title: Rename a Tracked Guide
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

Rename `docs/guide-draft.md` to `docs/volunteer-guide.md` with Git, then inspect short status.
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.hint: Move the tracked path through Git so the structural change is staged.

help.concept: git mv changes the working tree path and stages the removal/addition pair as one intentional rename.
help.hint_1: Confirm that the new path exists and the old path no longer appears as tracked.
help.hint_2: Review the staged name-status summary before committing.

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.solutionCode:
git mv docs/guide-draft.md docs/volunteer-guide.md
git status --short

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-kit
mkdir -p docs
printf %s '# Draft Guide
' > docs/guide-draft.md
cd ..
cd release-kit
mkdir -p docs
printf %s 'Old checklist
' > docs/old-checklist.md
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.title: Remove an Obsolete Checklist
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

Remove `docs/old-checklist.md` with Git and verify that the deletion is staged.
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.hint: Remove the obsolete tracked path through Git so the deletion is staged.

help.concept: git rm removes a tracked file from the working tree and stages that deletion for the next commit.
help.hint_1: Earlier commits will still preserve the old file.
help.hint_2: Verify the deletion alongside any related rename before recording the cleanup.

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.title: Review a Rename and Removal Together
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

A guide rename and obsolete-file removal are already staged. Use name-status output to review both changes before committing.

help.concept: A staged name-status view makes structural changes visible before they become history.
help.hint_1: Look for the old path, new path, and any deletion named in the task.
help.hint_2: No unrelated path should appear in the staged cleanup.

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.title: Commit the File Cleanup
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

The rename and removal are staged. Commit them as `Organize release documentation`, then verify the tracked files and clean state.
try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.hint: Record only the staged snapshot and use the message requested by the scenario.

help.concept: A focused cleanup commit records the project’s new file structure without rewriting earlier snapshots.
help.hint_1: The message should describe the project result, not the commands you used.
help.hint_2: After recording it, inspect history and any work that remains.

try_rename_and_remove_tracked_files_sket.solutionCode:
git commit -m "Organize release documentation"
git ls-files
git status --short

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of git mv
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git mv` command?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Use Git-aware structural operations, review the staged paths, and record a cleanup without losing earlier versions.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To move a file without tracking
options.b: To rename a file and stage the change

single-choice-2.title: Effect of git rm
single-choice-2.prompt: What happens when you use the `git rm` command?
single-choice-2.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Use Git-aware structural operations, review the staged paths, and record a cleanup without losing earlier versions.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: The file is moved to a new location
options.b: The file is removed from the working directory and staged for deletion

multi-choice-1.title: Commands to Verify Staged Changes
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which commands can you use to verify staged changes before committing?
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Use Git-aware structural operations, review the staged paths, and record a cleanup without losing earlier versions.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

drag-reorder-1.title: Steps to Rename and Commit a File
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps to rename a file and commit the change using Git.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Use Git-aware structural operations, review the staged paths, and record a cleanup without losing earlier versions.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

tokens.t1: Use git mv to rename the file
tokens.t2: Check the status with git status
tokens.t3: Commit the change with git commit

fill-blank-choice-1.title: Command for Renaming a File
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Complete the command to rename `file1.txt` to `file2.txt` using Git.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.concept: Use Git-aware structural operations, review the staged paths, and record a cleanup without losing earlier versions.
help.hint_1: Pay attention to whether the prompt describes an untracked path, a working-tree edit, staged work, or history.
help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

fill-blank-choice-2.hint: Choose the term or symbol that matches the exact state described, not a nearby Git concept.

help.hint_2: Read spaces, punctuation, and status columns as meaningful when the question shows Git output.

sketch0.title: Rename a Path Through Git
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
A tracked file can move without losing its story. A Git-aware rename updates the working tree and stages the old-path/new-path relationship in one step.

Git ultimately detects renames by comparing content, so a staged summary may show a similarity percentage rather than a permanent “rename object.” Keeping the content recognizable makes that relationship clear.

Always inspect both the new path and the disappearance of the old one before committing.

sketch1.title: Remove a Path Without Erasing History
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Removing a tracked file stages its deletion from the next snapshot and removes it from the working tree. Earlier commits still contain the file, so the project history remains intact.

This is different from ignoring a path. Use removal when the project should no longer contain the file; use ignore rules for untracked output that should stay local.

The staged report should make the deletion visible before it becomes history.

sketch2.title: Review the New Repository Shape
sketch2.bodyMarkdown:
Structural work is easier to review as names and statuses. A staged name-status report can show the renamed guide and the deleted checklist without burying the shape inside a long content patch.

Confirm three facts: the new path exists, the old path is gone, and no unrelated file is staged.

The goal is a repository whose current structure matches the story told by the next commit.

sketch3.title: Describe the Cleanup as a Result
sketch3.bodyMarkdown:
When a rename and removal serve the same cleanup goal, they can share one focused commit. The message should describe the result—such as organizing release documentation—not repeat the shell operations.

After committing, inspect the tracked file list and status. The new path should be tracked, the obsolete path should be absent, and no unfinished work should remain.

History preserves where the files came from even though the current snapshot has changed.
